title: John Marc DeMatteis
content:
{{Top|rwp}} {{Otheruses|John}} {{Person |image=[[File:Jm_dematteis.jpg]] |name=J. M. DeMatteis |birth=John Marc DeMatteis
[[December 15]], [[1950s|1953]]
[[Wikipedia:Brooklyn|Brooklyn, New York]] |death= |listedpronouns= |nationality= |occupation=Writer |companies=[[Marvel Comics]] |website= }} '''John Marc DeMatteis''' (born [[December 15]], [[1950s|1953]]) is a comic book writer, usually credited under the name '''J. M. DeMatteis'''. He wrote the story for [[Star Wars (1977) 46|''Star Wars'' (1977) 46]] under the pseudonym '''Wally Lombego''' due to changes made by [[Lucasfilm]] to his original plot. He wanted [[Lando Calrissian/Legends|Lando Calrissian]] to accept [[Cody Sunn-Childe]]'s pacifist views but Lucasfilm overruled him and had the ending changed. ==Biography== ===Early career=== DeMatteis began as a music critic before getting his start in comic books at [[Wikipedia:DC Comics|DC Comics]] in the late 1970s, when he contributed to the company's line of horror comics. He began writing for [[Marvel Comics]] in 1980 on the tiles [[Wikipedia:Defenders (comics)|''Defenders'']] and [[Wikipedia:Spider-Man|''Spider-Man'']], and had a lengthy run on ''Captain America'', paired with penciler [[Wikipedia:Mike Zeck|Mike Zeck]]. ===1980s=== In 1987, DeMatteis and Zeck re-teamed for the "[[Wikipedia:Kraven's Last Hunt|Kraven's Last Hunt]]" arc that ran throughout Marvel's then three [[Wikipedia:Spider-Man|Spider-Man]] titles. DeMatteis and illustrator [[Wikipedia:Jon J. Muth|Jon J. Muth]] created the graphic novel [[Wikipedia:Moonshadow (graphic novel)|''Moonshadow'']], the first fully-painted series in American comics for Marvel's Epic line. DeMatteis followed this with [[Wikipedia:Blood: A Tale|''Blood: A Tale'']], a hallucinatory vampire story drawn by [[Wikipedia:Kent Williams|Kent Williams]], and the 1986 Dr. Strange graphic novel [[Wikipedia:Into Shambhala|''Into Shambhala'']]. Moving back to DC, DeMatteis succeeded [[Wikipedia:Gerry Conway|Gerry Conway]] as writer of the superhero-team title [[Wikipedia:Justice League|''Justice League of America'']]. When that series was cancelled in the wake of the company-wide crossover [[Wikipedia:Crisis on Infinite Earths|''Crisis on Infinite Earths'']], DeMatteis stayed through its relaunch as [[Wikipedia:Justice League International|''Justice League International'']], scripting over the plots of [[Wikipedia:Keith Giffen|Keith Giffen]]. ''JLI'' took such lesser-known DC characters as Martian Manhunter, Blue Beetle, Booster Gold, Mister Miracle, Captain Atom, and Power Girl and turned the then-current preoccupation with "grim 'n' gritty" superheroes on its head. The lighthearted series emphasized the absurd aspects of people with strange powers, wearing colorful costumes, volunteering to fight evildoers. While the League had its serious side and often faced world-threatening villains, it also featured such characters as the lovably inept G'Nort, the worst Green Lantern in the [[Wikipedia:Green Lantern Corps|Corps]]; Mr. Nebula, the interplanetary decorator; the Injustice League, a bunch of bumbling losers; and a flock of homicidal penguins who had been hybridized with piranhas. ===1990s=== DeMatteis stayed with ''JLI'' for five years, often scripting its spin-offs, such as a Mister Miracle solo title, or a European branch of the Justice League. Back at Marvel, DeMatteis again succeeded Conway, this time as writer of [[Wikipedia:The Spectacular Spider-Man|''The Spectacular Spider-Man'']] in 1991, taking the series in a grimmer, more psychologically oriented direction. In collaboration with regular artist Sal Buscema, DeMatteis' story arc ''The Child Within'' featured the return of the Harry Osborn Green Goblin. Spider-Man's battle with the Goblin continued in ''The Osborn Legacy'' in #189 and came to an end when Harry was killed in ''The Best Of Enemies!''. In the mid-1990s, DeMatteis took over from David Michelinie as writer of [[Wikipedia:The Amazing Spider-Man|''The Amazing Spider-Man'']] for a run that included the apparent death of [[Wikipedia:Spider-Man|Peter Parker's]] Aunt May and the beginnings of the "Clone Saga" arc. DeMatteis as well worked on such characters as Doctor Strange, [[Wikipedia:Daredevil (Marvel Comics)|Daredevil]], Man-Thing, and the Silver Surfer. DeMatteis helped launch DC's mature-audience [[Wikipedia:Vertigo (DC Comics)|Vertigo]] imprint, writing the graphic novels ''Mercy'' and ''Farewell, Moonshadow'', the miniseries ''The Last One'', and the 15-issue series ''[[Wikipedia:Seekers Into The Mystery]]'', the story of a Hollywood screenwriter on a journey of self-discovery and the search for universal truths. DeMatteis contributed tales of [[Wikipedia:Superman|Superman]], Batman, Wonder Woman, and Doctor Fate; redefining the [[Wikipedia:Spectre (comics)|Spectre]], through the character of Hal Jordan, as a spirit of redemption rather than of vengeance; and in 2003, with Giffen, revived the Justice League International for the miniseries ''Formerly Known as the Justice League''. The series won Giffen, DeMatteis and artist Kevin Maguire an Eisner Award. The team followed this with "I Can't Believe It's Not The Justice League" arc in ''JLA Classified'' and, at Marvel, a five-issue run of ''The Defenders''. In 2006, DeMatteis and Giffen began work on two original superhero comedy series, ''Hero Squared'' and ''Planetary Brigade'' for Boom! Studios. ===21st century=== DeMatteis later teamed with veteran artist Mike Ploog to create the CrossGen fantasy comic ''Abadazad'' (May 2004). The following year, Ploog and DeMatteis announced they were collaborating on a five-issue miniseries, ''Stardust Kid'', from the Image Comics imprint Desperado Publishing. The series moved to Boom! Studios in 2006. ===Other media=== [[File:Mswc46cover.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Cover of [[Star Wars (1977) 46|''Star Wars'' (1977) 46]] written by DeMatteis]] DeMatteis has also written for television, having scripted episodes of the 1980s incarnation of [[Wikipedia:The Twilight Zone|''The Twilight Zone'']], the syndicated series [[Wikipedia:Superboy (TV series)|''The Adventures of Superboy'']] and ''Earth: Final Conflict'', as well as for the animated series ''The Real Ghostbusters'', ''Justice League Unlimited'' and ''Legion of Super-Heroes.'' He has written un-produced screenplays for Twentieth Century Fox, Disney Feature Animation and producer/directors [[Wikipedia:Chris Columbus (filmmaker)|Chris Columbus]] and Dean Devlin. ==Star Wars bibliography== *[[Star Wars (1977) 46|''Star Wars'' (1977) 46]] *''[[Star Wars: A Long Time Ago... Volume 3: Resurrection of Evil]]'' ==Sources== *{{WebCite|url=http://www.desperadopublishing.com/Creators/J.M.%20DeMatteis.htm|text=Profile of J. M. DeMatteis|work=Desperado Publishing|archivedate=20050428133454}} *''How to do Star Wars the Marvel Way'', by Glenn Greenberg, [[Wikipedia:Back Issue Magazine|Back Issue Magazine]] #9 ==External links== *{{WP|J. M. DeMatteis}} *{{WebCite|url=http://fanboyradio.libsyn.com/index.php?post_id=31051|text=Interview|work=Fanboy Radio|archivedate=20060329015423}} [[Category:Comic authors]] [[Category:Non-fiction authors]]
